steps.title: Plan the layout with guide marks
steps.description: Lightly sketch three small dots or rough placeholder shapes to reserve a spot for each object. Try the bottle on the left, the fruit in the center-front, and the book to the right and slightly behind. Getting placement right in pencil now means you won't have to start over later.

steps.title: Establish the ground plane and horizon
steps.description: Draw a light horizontal line for the surface the objects rest on. Just above it, mark a faint implied horizon — this gentle perspective will make the horizontal lines of the book converge very slightly toward it.

steps.title: Block in the three object silhouettes
steps.description: Sketch simple shapes for each object: a tall narrow rectangle for the bottle, a circle for the fruit, and a thin flattened rectangle for the book. Let the fruit overlap the base of the bottle slightly — that overlap is what creates depth.

steps.title: Refine each object's form
steps.description: Work through each shape in turn. Give the bottle a slight shoulder taper and an ellipse for its opening. Round the fruit into a sphere and add a small stem nub. Sketch the book's pages as a slight fan at the open spine and add a faint center crease.

steps.title: Establish a consistent light direction
steps.description: Choose one light source — upper left works well. On the right side of each object, add short parallel pencil strokes to suggest shadow: a vertical row along the bottle, a crescent curve along the lower right of the fruit, and diagonal strokes across the far half of the book pages.

steps.title: Add cast shadows on the surface
steps.description: Each object casts a shadow away from your light source. Draw the shadows as flattened ellipses or wedges on the table surface, darkest right at the base of each object and lighter as they extend outward.

steps.title: Vary detail density by depth
steps.description: Give the most detail to the closest object — the fruit — with a crisp edge and a clear highlight dot. The bottle can have a little less, and the book at the back gets the least rendering. This atmospheric detail drop-off makes the arrangement feel naturally three-dimensional.
